content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacrnmento, CA 95811 
(916) :122-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Daniel Sodergren 
P. 0. Box 520 
123 Main Street 
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-259 
Dear Mr. Sodergren: 
November 22, 2017 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of City of Pleasanton {"City") Economic 
Vitality Committee {"EVC") Member Olivia Sanwong for advice regarding the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act {the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Committee Member Sanwong attend and participate in meetings of the EVC related 
to the Downtown Specific Plan amendments? 
2. May Committee Member Sanwong attend and participate in meetings of the Downtown 
Specific Plan Task Force, Parks and Recreation Commission, Planning Commission, and City 
Council related to the Downtown Specific Plan amendments? 
CONCLUSIONS 
I & 2. The Act prohibits Committee Member Sanwong from making, participating in 
making, and influencing the decisions regarding the Downtown Specific Plan Update based on the 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-259 
Page No. 2 
facts presented. However, appearing before the Downtown Specific Plan Task Force, Parks and 
Recreation Commission, Planning Commission, and City Council would not be considered 
influencing the decisions, as contemplated by the Act, so long as Committee Member Sanwong 
does not act or purport to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, the EVC. 
FACTS 
Committee Member Sanwong serves on the City's EVC. Members of the EVC are subject to 
the City's conflict of interest code. The City is considering amendments to its Downtown Specific 
Plan, General Plan amendments for conformity with any such Downtown Specific Plan 
amendments, and related zoning code amendments for the same Downtown (collectively the 
"Downtown Specific Plan amendments"). 
The Downtown Specific Plan amendments are currently under consideration by the City's 
Downtown Specific Plan Update Task Force, Planning Commission, Parks and Recreation 
Commission, EVC, and City Council. The Downtown Specific Plan amendments under 
consideration were described as follows to the Pleasanton City Council on July 19, 2016: 
" ... reevaluating certain elements of the [Downtown Specific] plan to: 
• Clarify areas of policy direction that are vague, 
• More effectively respond to market factors and community objectives, 
• Strengthen the protection of physical features of Downtown that are valued by 
the community, and 
• Incorporate key concepts and principles from recent long-term planning efforts. 
Initially, DSP [Downtown Specific Plan] topics that were targeted for an update 
or clarification included: integration of residential development, public parking, 
and the Office land use district. After receiving informal feedback from the 
community, this list expanded to include potential topics for consideration such 
as: parking requirements for development projects; second units (or accessory 
dwelling units); maximum building height in Downtown for residential and 
commercial properties; clarification of maximum building height for residential 
and commercial properties; trail/pathway integration; Division Street 
enhancements; integration of recent long-term planning efforts ( e.g., Civic Center 
Master Plan); and green design features." 
The Downtown Specific Plan Update Task Force has ten representatives comprised of: 
two councilmembers and one alternate; two planning commissioners and one alternate; two 
Pleasanton Downtown Association members and one alternate; one EVC member and one 
alternate; and three at-large members. 
Committee Member Sanwong owns and lives in residential property that is located on 
Division Street, within the Downtown Specific Plan area. She is not a formal applicant before the 
City, but rather her home will only be effected in a similar manner to other residential property if 
any applicable Downtown Specific Plan amendments are adopted. Division Street enhancements 
are also specifically noted as potential topics for consideration in the Downtown Specific Plan. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-17-259 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a 
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. They include, as pertinent to your facts, any real 
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 
87103(b ). ) The Commission has defined "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a 
governmental decision in Regulation 18704 as follows: 
• A public official "makes a governmental decision" if the official authorizes or directs any 
action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of 
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18704(a).) 
• A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" if the official provides 
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision 
without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).) 
• 
Public officials are also prohibited from "influencing" a governmental decision. There are 
two rules as to whether a public official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision. (Regulation 18704( c).) 
The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public 
official's own agency or "an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her 
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision." In these cases, if the official "contacts or appears 
before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the 
agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a governmental decision. (Regulation 
18704(c)(l ).) 
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency other 
than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control 
of the public official's agency. (Regulation l 8704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or 
purport "to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact" 
to influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his or her economic interests. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly" or 
"not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly 
involved, a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) A financial interest is 
explicitly involved when it is a named party or the "subject" of the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Regulation 18702.Z(a)(l )  provides that a decision has a material financial effect on property 
when the decision concerns the adoption of or amendment to a general or specific plan, and the 


File No. A-17-259 
Page No. 4 
parcel is located within the proposed boundaries of the plan. Committee Member Sanwong owns 
and lives in residential property that is located on Division Street, within the Downtown Specific 
Plan area, and on a street that is specifically identified as a location that would potentially receive 
enhancements under the Downtown Specific Plan. Therefore, she will have a conflict of interest and 
may not make, participate in, or use her positon to influence any decisions to amend the Downtown 
Specific Plan. Accordingly, Committee Member Sanwong must recuse herself from any decisions 
before the EVC and may not contact members of the EVC or other city employees regarding any 
decision that is still pending before the EVC. 2 
Decisions of Other Agencies 
The Downtown Specific Plan Task Force, Parks and Recreation Commission, Planning 
Commission, and City Council are not appointed by or under the budgetary control of the EVC and 
fall under Regulation 18704(b ), set forth above. Thus, so long as the Commissioner does not act or 
purport to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, the EVC, she would be able to attend and 
participate in the meetings held by the Downtown Specific Plan Task Force, Parks and Recreation 
Commission, Planning Commission, and City Council. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
;J 
Zachary W. Norton 
By: 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
2 When staff is shared for a project which is, or will be, before the official's agency, meeting or othenvise 
comm1111icming with the sl,a,.ed staff regarding the project is cm appearance orcom1111111ication with the official's own 
agency. (Farrell Advice Letter, No. 1-03-121, emphasis added.)
